abstract:
The cryogenic separation of noble gases is both energy-intensive and costly, particularly when processing low-concentration streams. While metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) functionalized with polarizing groups are predicted to be superior solid-state adsorbents for Xe/Kr separation, experimental evidence regarding the fundamental nature of the xenon–framework interaction has remained elusive. In this study, we report a new microporous calcium-based MOF, designated SBMOF-2, which demonstrates high selectivity for Xe over Kr under ambient conditions. At 298 K, SBMOF-2 exhibits a Xe/Kr selectivity of approximately 10 and a significant xenon adsorption capacity of 27.07 wt %. 

Single-crystal X-ray diffraction reveals that this selectivity is driven by unique pore geometries—specifically, cages composed of phenyl rings and enriched with polar -OH groups. These features provide robust adsorption sites that preferentially interact with the more polarizable xenon atoms. We further validated the separation performance through both computational modeling and experimental breakthrough studies. In these trials, krypton eluted from the column well before xenon, confirming that SBMOF-2 possesses significant practical potential for industrial gas separation. Computational benchmarks indicate that the adsorption capacity and selectivity of SBMOF-2 are competitive with the best-performing unmodified MOFs, such as NiMOF-74 and cobalt formate, while offering a more stable, lightweight framework.
